title: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 7: Soteriology - The Doctrine of Salvation
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 7 - Soteriology_ The Doctrine of Salvation - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction to Soteriology: The Order of Salvation
subpoints: Salvation in Scripture refers to the entire journey from deadness in sin to glorification, not just a moment of belief

The order of salvation (ordo salutis): effectual calling, regeneration, repentance, faith, justification, definitive sanctification, adoption, progressive sanctification, perseverance, and glorification
Some steps are monergistic (God alone acts); others are synergistic (God and human response work together)
Today's focus: the part of redemption accomplished - election and reprobation

outline.title: God's Character as the Foundation
subpoints: Exodus 34:6-7 defines God as merciful, gracious, long-suffering, and abounding in goodness and truth
The doctrine of election must be understood in light of this God - not a capricious God but a gracious, covenantal one
The goal is not to make God conform to our image but to let God be who He is as declared in Scripture
Election is the decree of this God, whose goodness and severity are both revealed in the same text

outline.title: Unconditional Election
subpoints: God chose a group of people solely to be saved based on His will alone, not anything foreseen in them
Romans 9:19-23: humanity is as passive and inert as a lump of clay; the potter shapes some for honor and some for dishonor from the same lump
There is no injustice in God doing this, because mercy itself is something nobody deserves (Romans 9:14-16)
Election produces humility: our salvation is entirely the grace of God, nothing in us prompted Him to save us

outline.title: Irresistible Grace and Particular Redemption
subpoints: Irresistible grace: God's saving grace is all-powerful and cannot ultimately be resisted; those the Father gives to the Son will come (John 6:37-44)
Jesus says 'my sheep will hear my voice' - not 'I hope they hear,' but they will hear and come (John 10:11-27)
Particular redemption: Christ died for a definite group; all for whom He died will be saved and raised at the last day
The theological necessity: if Christ died for everyone equally and some are in hell, God punished His Son and then punished them again for the same sins

outline.title: Reprobation and the Purposes of These Doctrines
subpoints: God has not only elected people to salvation but has also appointed some to condemnation - Romans 9 teaches this from the same lump of clay
Paul anticipates the question of fairness and answers: 'Who are you, O man, to answer back to God?' The challenge is both irrelevant and irreverent
The purposes of the doctrine of election: it causes praise (Romans 11:33), it gives confidence (Romans 8:38), and it produces humility
Our confession: this high mystery is to be handled with special prudence and care, affirming certainty of election through certainty of one's effectual calling

summary:
This session opens the Foundations series' final category: Soteriology, the Doctrine of Salvation. Pastor Lyro begins by distinguishing the breadth of the word 'salvation' in Scripture - it encompasses the entire journey from election in eternity past through glorification at the resurrection, not merely a single moment of belief. He introduces the classical 'order of salvation' (ordo salutis): effectual calling, regeneration, repentance, faith, justification, definitive sanctification, adoption, progressive sanctification, perseverance, and glorification. Some of these are monergistic (God alone acting), others synergistic (God and the human response), but all are ultimately the result of God's grace.

Before addressing the doctrine of election, Lyro anchors the discussion in Exodus 34:6-7, where God reveals Himself to Moses as merciful, gracious, long-suffering, and abounding in goodness and truth. This is the God who has decreed election and reprobation - not a capricious or arbitrary deity but one who keeps mercy for thousands while holding the guilty accountable. With this foundation, Lyro presents unconditional election from Romans 9: humanity is as passive and inert as clay in the potter's hands, and the potter shapes some for honor and some for dishonor from the same lump with no difference in the clay itself to determine the outcome. There is no injustice in this because mercy is something nobody deserves.

The session then defends irresistible grace from John 6 and John 10, where Jesus repeatedly speaks of those the Father gives Him as those who will (not might) come to Him and be raised at the last day. From John 17, Jesus explicitly distinguishes praying for His own from praying for the world, confirming a definite group. Particular redemption follows logically: if Christ died for every human being equally and some are in hell, then God punished His Son for their sins and then punished them again - a theological impossibility given God's character.

The session closes with a careful treatment of reprobation - that God has actively ordained some to condemnation - drawing from Romans 9. Paul's response to the objection of unfairness is that the challenge is both irrelevant and irreverent: the infinite God cannot be fully comprehended by finite minds. These doctrines, when properly understood, produce praise, assurance, and humility rather than cold theological abstraction.
transcript:
## Introduction

Those of us in this class will continue what we began a couple of months ago — our class on foundations: the things foundational to the Christian faith, the things that are important for us to revisit. We have looked at four areas so far: creation, providence, what the Bible teaches concerning humanity, and what the Bible teaches concerning itself — the Word of God. I did not cover the doctrine of God because on Wednesday nights we had recently done that.

Today we are entering the last area of our study on foundations. It is the last area, not the last lesson. It is the doctrine of salvation — called soteriology in books of theology, from the Greek word for salvation and Savior. There are very few things more important for us to know — and to know well — than what the Bible teaches concerning our redemption.

We are going to follow a classic approach, talking about redemption in two areas. Theologians have historically divided this category into two areas: redemption accomplished (predestination, the work of Christ on the cross — what God has done to save people) and redemption applied (the process of salvation for each individual — how people come to know God and how God works that out in their lives). That is the plan for this last phase of our study.

## What Is Salvation?

We use the word salvation in common speech to mean that particular moment when we came to believe in Jesus Christ and were saved. The Bible does not necessarily use the word in that way. Salvation in the Bible describes the entire journey from complete unbelief and deadness in sin to being finally glorified with Jesus in the new heavens and new earth. It is a big, comprehensive word.

## Redemption Accomplished: Election

The first area under redemption accomplished is election. What is election? It is God choosing, before the foundation of the world, those who will be saved. Ephesians 1:4–5:

> *"Just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love, having predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to Himself, according to the good pleasure of His will."* (Ephesians 1:4–5)

The elect are those whom God chose in Christ before the foundation of the world. There is a second category in the decree of God, which is preterition or reprobation — God passing over those who are not elect and allowing them to receive the just punishment for their sins. This is a difficult doctrine, but it is in the Bible. Romans 9:

> *"For He says to Moses, 'I will have mercy on whomever I will have mercy, and I will have compassion on whomever I will have compassion.' So then it is not of him who wills, nor of him who runs, but of God who shows mercy... Therefore He has mercy on whom He wills, and whom He wills He hardens."* (Romans 9:15–16, 18)

Is this fair? Paul anticipates the objection: "O man, who are you to reply against God? Will the thing formed say to him who formed it, 'Why have you made me like this?'" (Romans 9:20). The question of God's fairness assumes that all sinners deserve something positive from God — but they do not. The question should be: why does God show mercy to any sinners at all? The wonder is not that some are not elected; the wonder is that any are.

## The Covenant of Redemption

Before time began, the three persons of the Trinity entered into what theologians call the covenant of redemption or the counsel of peace. The Father gave a people to the Son. The Son agreed to take on human nature, live a perfect life, and die in the place of his people. The Holy Spirit agreed to apply the work of the Son to those given to him. John 6:37–40:

> *"All that the Father gives Me will come to Me, and the one who comes to Me I will by no means cast out. For I have come down from heaven, not to do My own will, but the will of Him who sent Me. This is the will of the Father who sent Me, that of all He has given Me I should lose nothing, but should raise it up at the last day. And this is the will of Him who sent Me, that everyone who sees the Son and believes in Him may have everlasting life."* (John 6:37–40)

The covenant of redemption is the foundation of our salvation. Because the Father gave certain people to the Son, and because the Son agreed to save them, those people will absolutely come to faith and be saved. Not one of them will be lost.

## The Atonement: Its Nature and Extent

The atonement is the work of Christ on the cross. What did Christ do on the cross? He paid the penalty for the sins of his people — his death was a substitutionary, propitiatory sacrifice. It satisfied the righteous wrath of God against sin, and it reconciled God to his people.

The extent of the atonement: for whom did Christ die? This is a disputed question among Christians. The general position in the Reformed tradition is that the atonement is sufficient for all but efficient — actually saving — only those whom the Father gave to the Son. John 10:14–15, 26–28:

> *"I am the good shepherd; and I know My sheep, and am known by My own. As the Father knows Me, even so I know the Father; and I lay down My life for the sheep."* (John 10:14–15)

> *"But you do not believe, because you are not of My sheep, as I said to you. My sheep hear My voice, and I know them, and they follow Me. And I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; neither shall anyone snatch them out of My hand."* (John 10:26–28)

Jesus says he lays down his life for the sheep — not for all without distinction, but for those who are his. This is consistent with the covenant of redemption: the Son agreed to die for those the Father gave him, and those people will certainly be saved.

## Union With Christ

A foundational concept for understanding all of redemption is union with Christ. The elect are united to Christ before the foundation of the world — chosen in him (Ephesians 1:4). They are united to him in his death: Romans 6:3–5 says that all who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death. We are united to him in his resurrection. And we are united to him in his ongoing intercession for us.

This union with Christ is the basis for everything else in redemption. It is because we are in Christ that his righteousness is credited to us. It is because we are in Christ that his Spirit is given to us. It is because we are in Christ that we cannot be separated from the love of God.

## The Order of Salvation: Overview

After redemption accomplished, we move to redemption applied — how God actually saves each individual. Theologians call this the order of salvation, or the ordo salutis. The ten steps we will cover are:

1. Effectual calling
2. Regeneration
3. Repentance unto life
4. Faith in Jesus Christ
5. Justification
6. Definitive justification
7. Adoption
8. Progressive sanctification
9. Perseverance in holiness
10. Glorification

These are logical steps, not necessarily chronological steps — many happen at the same time, or are even logically simultaneous. Some are acts of God that happen in a moment. Some are works of God that happen over a period of time. The most obvious ongoing work of God is progressive sanctification.

The key thing to understand about the order of salvation is that it is all of grace. God initiates, God applies, God preserves, God completes. The entire order of salvation is the result of God's grace — it is not of ourselves, lest any man should boast.

## Summary

The doctrine of salvation teaches us that God alone saves, and he saves through the proclamation of the gospel. His salvation starts with election in eternity past and concludes with our glorification in the new heavens and new earth. In between, every step — from the new birth to perseverance — is the work of God's sovereign grace, accomplished by the Lord Jesus Christ and applied by the Holy Spirit.
